revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 識名宮 |画像 = [[画像:Nippon Okinawa Naha 識名宮.jpg|300px]] |所在地 = 沖縄県那覇市繁多川4-1-43 |位置 = |祭神 = 伊弉冉尊
速玉男尊
事解男尊
午ぬふぁ神
識名権現 |社格 = 旧無格社 |創建 = 尚元王朝（1556年～1572年） |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = 琉球八社 |例祭 = 9月15日 |神事 = }} '''識名宮'''（しきなぐう）は、[[沖縄県]][[那覇市]]にある[[神社]]。[[琉球八社]]の一つで、[[明治]]の[[近代社格制度]]では[[無格社]]。 == 由緒 == [[尚元王]]の長男の病気回復に霊験があったので、識名宮を創建した。[[沖縄戦]]で焼失したが、[[戦後]]に再建された。 == 祭神 == ;熊野権現 *[[伊弉冉尊]] *[[速玉男命]] *[[事解男命]] *午ぬふぁ神 *識名権現 == 関連項目 == *[[琉球神道]] {{DEFAULTSORT:しきなくう}} [[Category:沖縄県の神社]] [[Category:熊野神社]] [[Category:沖縄県の旧無格社]] [[Category:那覇市]] {{shinto-stub}}

{{神社 |名称 = 識名宮 |画像 = [[画像:Nippon Okinawa Naha 識名宮.jpg|300px]] |所在地 = 沖縄県那覇市繁多川4-1-43 |位置 = |祭神 = 伊弉冉尊
速玉男尊
事解男尊
午ぬふぁ神
識名権現 |社格 = 旧無格社 |創建 = 尚元王朝（1556年～1572年） |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = 琉球八社 |例祭 = 9月15日 |神事 = }} '''識名宮'''（しきなぐう）は、[[沖縄県]][[那覇市]]にある[[神社]]。[[琉球八社]]の一つで、[[明治]]の[[近代社格制度]]では[[無格社]]。 == 祭神 == ;熊野権現 *[[伊弉冉尊]] *[[速玉男命]] *[[事解男命]] *午ぬふぁ神 *識名権現 == 由緒 == [[尚元王]]の長男の病気回復に霊験があったので、識名宮を創建した。[[沖縄戦]]で焼失したが、[[戦後]]に再建された。 == 関連項目 == *[[琉球神道]] {{DEFAULTSORT:しきなくう}} [[Category:沖縄県の神社]] [[Category:熊野神社]] [[Category:沖縄県の旧無格社]] [[Category:那覇市]] {{shinto-stub}}

{{出典の明記|date=2011年3月}} {{神社 |名称 = 識名宮 |画像 = [[画像:Nippon Okinawa Naha 識名宮.jpg|300px]] |所在地 = 沖縄県那覇市繁多川4-1-43 |位置 = |祭神 = 伊弉冉尊
速玉男尊
事解男尊
午ぬふぁ神
識名権現 |社格 = 旧無格社 |創建 = 尚元王朝（1556年～1572年） |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = 琉球八社 |例祭 = 9月15日 |神事 = }} '''識名宮'''（しきなぐう）は、[[沖縄県]][[那覇市]]にある[[神社]]。[[琉球八社]]の一つで、[[明治]]の[[近代社格制度]]では[[無格社]]。 == 祭神 == ;熊野権現 *[[伊弉冉尊]] *[[速玉男命]] *[[事解男命]] *午ぬふぁ神 *識名権現 == 由緒 == [[尚元王]]の長男の病気回復に霊験があったので、識名宮を創建した。[[沖縄戦]]で焼失したが、[[戦後]]に再建された。 == 関連項目 == *[[琉球神道]] {{DEFAULTSORT:しきなくう}} [[Category:沖縄県の神社]] [[Category:熊野神社]] [[Category:沖縄県の旧無格社]] [[Category:那覇市]] {{shinto-stub}}

{{神社 |名称 = 識名宮 |画像 = [[画像:Nippon Okinawa Naha 識名宮.jpg|300px]] |所在地 = 沖縄県那覇市繁多川4-1-43 |位置 = |祭神 = 伊弉冉尊
速玉男尊
事解男尊
午ぬふぁ神
識名権現 |社格 = 旧無格社 |創建 = 尚元王朝（1556年～1572年） |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = 琉球八社 |例祭 = 9月15日 |神事 = }} '''識名宮'''（しきなぐう）は、[[沖縄県]][[那覇市]]にある[[神社]]。[[琉球八社]]の一つで、[[明治]]の[[近代社格制度]]では[[無格社]]。 == 祭神 == ;熊野権現 *[[伊弉冉尊]] *[[速玉男命]] *[[事解男命]] *午ぬふぁ神 *識名権現 == 由緒 == [[尚元王]]の長男の病気回復に霊験があったので、識名宮を創建したとの記述が「遺老説伝」にある。加治順人『沖縄の神社』p24　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 [[沖縄戦]]で焼失したが、[[昭和43年]]に再建された加治順人『沖縄の神社』p145　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 。 == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == *[[琉球神道]] {{DEFAULTSORT:しきなくう}} [[Category:沖縄県の神社]] [[Category:熊野神社]] [[Category:沖縄県の旧無格社]] [[Category:那覇市]] {{shinto-stub}}

識名権現 |社格 = 旧無格社 |創建 = 尚元王朝（1556年～1572年） |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = 琉球八社 |例祭 = 9月15日 |神事 = }} '''識名宮'''（しきなぐう）は、[[沖縄県]][[那覇市]]にある[[神社]]。[[琉球八社]]の一つで、[[明治]]の[[近代社格制度]]では[[無格社]]。 == 祭神 == ;熊野権現 *[[伊弉冉尊]] *[[速玉男命]] *[[事解男命]] *午ぬふぁ神 *識名権現 == 由緒 == [[尚元王]]の長男の病気回復に霊験があったので、識名宮を創建したとの記述が「遺老説伝」にある。加治順人『沖縄の神社』p24　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 [[沖縄戦]]で焼失したが、[[1968年]]に再建された加治順人『沖縄の神社』p145　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 。 == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} *[[琉球神道]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しきなくう}} [[Category:沖縄県の神社]] [[Category:那覇市]] [[Category:熊野神社]] [[Category:沖縄県の旧無格社]]

revision.comment: Created page with " == Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞..."
revision.text: == Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina Shrine|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi Shrine|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku Shrine|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}}

== Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina Shrine|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi Shrine|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku Shrine|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}}

However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}}

{{Single source|date=2014年2月5日 (水) 03:36 (UTC)}} {{Shrine | Name = Shikina Shrine | Image = [[File:識名宮拝殿 - DJBzmAcUEAAL7HT.jpg|250px]] | Location = 4-1-43 Shikina, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], JapanShikina Shrine. [http://sikinagu.com/f.html Access]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27). | Latitude degrees = 26 | Latitude minutes = 12 | Latitude seconds = 36.7 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 127 | Longitude minutes = 42 | Longitude seconds = 48.3 | E/W = E | Country code = JP | Map = Japan Naha city | Deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]
Uma Nufa deity
Shikina Gongen | Shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | Founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | Festival = September 15 | Rituals = }} '''Shikina Shrine''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina Shrine. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina Shrine was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina Shrine Official Website] {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina Shrine}} [[Category:Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] == Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina Shrine Official Website] {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:Shikina-gū]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]] == Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina Shrine|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi Shrine|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku Shrine|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]][[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]]{{commonscat|Shikina-gū}}[[en:Shikina-gū]]

In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina Shrine|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi Shrine|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku Shrine|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]][[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]]{{commonscat|Shikina-gū}}[[en:Shikina-gū]]

Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina Shrine''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina Shrine. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina Shrine was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina Shrine Official Website] {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Shikina-gū]]== Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina Shrine|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi Shrine|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku Shrine|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]][[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}}[[en:Shikina-gū]]

In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina Shrine|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi Shrine|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku Shrine|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina Shrine''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina Shrine. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina Shrine was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina Shrine Official Website] {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Shikina-gū]]== Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina Shrine|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi Shrine|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku Shrine|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}}[[en:Shikina-gū]]

Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina Shrine''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina Shrine. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina Shrine was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina Shrine Official Website] {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Shikina-gū]]== Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}}[[en:Shikina-gū]]

Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina Shrine''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina Shrine. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina Shrine was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina Shrine Official Website] {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Shikina-gū]] [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}}[[en:Shikina-gū]]

In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]
Uma Nufa deity
Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina Shrine''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina Shrine. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina Shrine was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina Shrine Official Website] {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Shikina-gū]] [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Articles tagged as templates and modules]] {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}}[[en:Shikina-gū]]

In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Articles tagged as templates and modules]] {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}}[[en:Shikina-gū]]

{{Single source|date=2014年2月5日 (水) 03:36 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shikina Shrine | image = [[File:識名宮拝殿 - DJBzmAcUEAAL7HT.jpg|250px]] | location = 4-1-43 Shikina, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], JapanShikina Shrine. [http://sikinagu.com/f.html Access]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27). | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 36.7 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | Longitude minutes = 42 | Longitude seconds = 48.3 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]
Uma Nufa deity
Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina Shrine''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina Shrine. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina Shrine was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina Shrine Official Website] {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Shikina-gū]] [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Articles tagged as templates and modules]] {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}}[[en:Shikina-gū]]

In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Articles tagged as templates and modules]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]][[en:Shikina-gū]]{{commonscat|Shikina-gū}}[[en:Shikina-gū]]

{{Single source|date=2014年2月5日 (水) 03:36 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shikina Shrine | image = [[File:識名宮拝殿 - DJBzmAcUEAAL7HT.jpg|250px]] | location = 4-1-43 Shikina, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], JapanShikina Shrine. [http://sikinagu.com/f.html Access]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27). | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 36.7 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 48.3 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]
Uma Nufa deity
Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina Shrine''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina Shrine. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina Shrine was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina Shrine Official Website] {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Shikina-gū]] [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Articles tagged as templates and modules]] [[en:Shikina-gū]]{{commonscat|Shikina-gū}}[[en:Shikina-gū]]

It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina Shrine. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina Shrine was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina Shrine Official Website] {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Shikina-gū]] [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Articles tagged as templates and modules]] [[en:Shikina-gū]]{{commonscat|Shikina-gū}}[[en:Shikina-gū]]

In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[en:Shikina-gū]]{{commonscat|Shikina-gū}}[[en:Shikina-gū]]

{{Single source|date=2014年2月5日 (水) 03:36 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shikina Shrine | image = [[File:識名宮拝殿 - DJBzmAcUEAAL7HT.jpg|250px]] | location = 4-1-43 Shikina, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], JapanShikina Shrine. [http://sikinagu.com/f.html Access]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27). | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 36.7 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 48.3 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]
Uma Nufa deity
Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina Shrine''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina Shrine. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina Shrine was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina Shrine Official Website] {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Shikina-gū]] [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[en:Shikina-gū]]{{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} [[Category:ファイル]] [[en:Shikina-gū]]
