{{Single source|date=2014年2月5日 (水) 03:36 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shikina-gū | image = [[File:識名宮拝殿 - DJBzmAcUEAAL7HT.jpg|250px]] | location = 4-1-43 Shikina, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], JapanShikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/f.html Access]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27). | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 36.7 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 48.3 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]
Uma Nufa deity
Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] [[Category:new pages]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Shikina-gū]] [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|qq=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|qq=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|qq=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|qq=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|qq=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|qq=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|qq=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa and forks]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa and forks]] {{Single source|date=2014年2月5日 (水) 03:36 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shikina-gū | image = [[File:識名宮拝殿 - DJBzmAcUEAAL7HT.jpg|250px]] | location = 4-1-43 Shikina, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], JapanShikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/f.html Access]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27). | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 36.7 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 48.3 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]
Uma Nufa deity
Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] [[Category:new pages]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Shikina-gū]] [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|qq=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|qq=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|qq=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|qq=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|qq=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|qq=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|qq=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa and forks]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa and forks]] 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Shikina-gū]]{{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqq]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:qq]] In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|qq=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|qq=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|qq=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|qq=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|qq=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Pages to be written {{Single source|date=2014年2月5日 (水) 03:36 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shikina-gū | image = [[File:識名宮拝殿 - DJBzmAcUEAAL7HT.jpg|250px]] | location = 4-1-43 Shikina, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], JapanShikina-gū. Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Shikina-gū]] [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|qq=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|qq=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|qq=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|qq=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|qq=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|qq=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|qq=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Pages to be written Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Shikina-gū]] [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|qq=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|qq=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|qq=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|qq=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|qq=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|qq=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|qq=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Pages to be written {{Single source|date=2014年2月5日 (水) 03:36 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shikina-gū | image = [[File:識名宮拝殿 - DJBzmAcUEAAL7HT.jpg|250px]] | location = 4-1-43 Shikina, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], JapanShikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/f.html Access]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27). | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 36.7 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 48.3 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]
Uma Nufa deity
Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Shikina-gū]] [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Shikina-gū]]{{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[en:Shikina-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]]

Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Shikina-gū]]{{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[en:Shikina-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]]

Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Shikina-gū]]{{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[Category:Pages to be writtenaShinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]]

(Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Shikina-gū]]{{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[Category:Pages to be writtenaShinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]]

Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However,[[ja:識名宮]] after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Shikina-gū]]{{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]]

Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Shikina-gū]]{{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]]

Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Shikina-gū]]{{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]]

However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}}

{{Single source|date=2014th year 2月5日 (水) 03:36 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shikina-gū | image = [[File:識名宮拝殿 - DJBzmAcUEAAL7HT.jpg|250px]] | location = 4-1-43 Shikina, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], JapanShikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/f.html Access]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27). | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 36.7 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 48.3 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]
Uma Nufa deity
Shikina Gongen | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|12=draft}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|12=draft}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|12=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|12=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|12=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=draft}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=draft}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=draft}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=draft}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[en:Shikina-gū]] [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikina-gū}}
