Shikina Gongen | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|識名宮|version=94116673|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]]

It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|識名宮|version=94116673|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]]

(Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|識名宮|version=94116673|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]]

Shikina Gongen | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] *[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|識名宮|version=94116673|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]]

Shikina Gongen | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] *[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|識名宮|version=94116673|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]]

Shikina Gongen | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] *[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|識名宮|version=94116673|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]]

However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|識名宮|version=94116673|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11632260]] Wikidata name: Shikina-gū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Single source|date=2014th year 2月5日 (水) 03:36 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shikina-gū | image = File:Nippon Okinawa Naha 識名宮.jpg | location = 4-1-43 Shikina, [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], JapanShikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/f.html Access]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27). | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 36.7 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 48.3 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]]
[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]
Uma Nufa deity
Shikina Gongen | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = | location = {{ill|Naha|qid=Q181966}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.210211|N|127.713442|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|qid=Q11573652}} }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] *[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|識名宮|version=94116673|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Shikina-gū]][[sn:Shikina-gū]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[sn:Shikina-gū]] [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[sn:hikina-gū]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]]

(Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] *[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|識名宮|version=94116673|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11632260}}

Shikina Gongen | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = | location = {{ill|Naha|qid=Q181966}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.210211|N|127.713442|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|qid=Q11573652}} }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. Shikina Gongen | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = | location = {{ill|Naha|WD=Q181966}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.210211|N|127.713442|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|WD=Q11573652}} }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] *[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|識名宮|version=94116673|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11632260}}

However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|識名宮|version=94116673|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11632260}}

[[sn:commons:Category:Shikina-gū]][[ja:識名宮]][[zh:識名宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11632260]] Wikidata name: Shikina-gū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Single source|date=2014th year 2月5日 (水) 03:36 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shikina-gū | image = File:Nippon Okinawa Naha 識名宮.jpg | location = 4-1-43 Shikina, [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], JapanShikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/f.html Access]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27). | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 36.7 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 48.3 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]]
[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]
Uma Nufa deity
Shikina Gongen | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = | location = {{ill|Naha|WD=Q181966}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.210211|N|127.713442|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|WD=Q11573652}} }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] *[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|識名宮|version=94116673|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11632260}}

In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|識名宮|version=94116673|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11632260}}

In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|識名宮|version=94116673|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11632260}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[http://sikinagu.com/f.html Access]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27). | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 36.7 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 48.3 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]]

Uma Nufa deity
Shikina Gongen | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = Reign of King Shō Gen (1556–1572) | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = September 15 | rituals = | location = {{ill|Naha|WD=Q181966}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.210211|N|127.713442|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|WD=Q11573652}} }} '''Shikina-gū''' (識名宮, Shikina-gū) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]Shikina-gū. [http://sikinagu.com/a.html History]. (Retrieved 2017-09-27).. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] *[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *Uma Nufa deity *Shikina Gongen == History == According to "Irosetsuden," Shikina-gū was established after a divine miracle healed the eldest son of King Shō GenJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 24, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} The shrine was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]] but was rebuilt in 1968Junhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 145, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Shikina-gū}} * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == External Links == * [http://sikinagu.com/ Shikina-gū Official Website] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Shikina-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|識名宮|version=94116673|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[ja:識名宮]] [[zh:%E8%AD%98%E5%90%8D%E5%AE%AE]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{Commons category|Shikina-gū}} [[zh:識名宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11632260}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]
